title: practical english usage - Part 155
content:
- I'm really sorry.

Yes, well, I always DO  say thank-you.

- Polite people always say thank-you.

She certainly HAS made him  angry!

- She has certainly made him  angry.

position adverbials before them instead of after. Compare: When we  emphasise auxiliary verbs or am/are/is/was/were, we  put most mid­ 3 mid-position        (details):    adverbials        with    emphatic       verbs I probably won't be there.

Only one position is possible before a contracted negative.

He probably does not know, (     He does probably not know.) or I probably will not be there, (   I will probably not be there.) verb; they always come  before do.

When  adverbials come  before not, they may also come  before the first auxiliary / really don't like her. (strong dislike) I don't really like her. (mild dislike) meaning. Compare: Both positions are possible with some adverbials, often with a difference of I certainly do not agree. I do not often have headaches.

the negative; otherwise they come  after. Compare: In negative sentences, adverbials generally come before not if they emphasise 2 mid-position        (details):    adverbials        with    negative       verbs 1 don't trust politicians. I never have, and 1 never will.

'Are you working? '1 certainly am.' a mid-position adverbial comes before it.

When  an auxiliary verb is used alone instead of a complete verb phrase (►   279), Section16 Adverbs     and   Adverbials grammar •  201   manner, place and time Finally, eventually, already, soon and last can also go in mid-position.

Today I'm going to London. Every week she has a new hairstyle.

the message.

Front position is also common if the adverbial is not the main focus of She has a new hairstyle every week.

I'm going to London today. What did you do afterwards?

already, soon, still, last, daily, weekly, every year Examples: today, afterwards, in June, last year, finally, before, eventually, 3 time    and   definite      frequency The children are running around upstairs.

Adverbials of direction (movement) come  before adverbials of position.

not                                                                          not Here it comes. (     Here comes it.) There she is. (     There is she.) Pronoun subjects come  directly after here and there.

There's Alice. There goes our train!

Here comes your bus. (     Here your bus comes.) Here comes and There goes.

Here and there often begin clauses. Note the word order in Here/There is, On  the grass sat an enormous frog. Down came the rain.

the subject (►  271.1).

not the main focus of the message. In this case the verb often comes before Front position is also possible, especially in literary writing and if the adverbial is She's sitting at the end of the garden.

Don't throw orange peel out of the window.

The children are playing upstairs. Come and sit here.

Examples: upstairs, around, here, to bed, in London, out of the window 2 place The driver has been seriously injured Mid-position (after all auxiliary verbs) is especially common with passive verbs.

She angrily tore up the letter.  I slowly began to feel better again.

of the message.

Adverbials in -ly can also go in mid-position if the adverb is not the main focus She read the notice slowly.  Jack works really hard.

He drove off angrily.     You speak English well.

quietly, hard, softly Examples: angrily, happily, fast, slowly, well, badly, nicely, noisily, Adverbials of manner say how  something happens or is done.

order.

Adverbials of manner, place and time usually go in end position, often in that 201 manner,      place    and   time grammar •  202  adverbials modifying adverbials: terribly sorry; right past me Almost can also go in mid-position (►    198.3).

I'm really tired today. She walked right past me.

He threw the ball almost over the house.

I'll see you in the pub just before eight o'clock.

I'm terribly sorry about last night.

Everybody was extremely annoyed with Julian.

We all thought she sang very well.

These adverbials go directly before the words that they modify.
